draft.introduction: Matthew 7:13–29 contains the conclusion of the Sermon on the Mount, where Jesus contrasts the narrow gate with the wide gate. From Codex Sinaiticus, fourth century.

translation.text: Enter through the narrow gate, for wide is the gate and broad is the road that leads to destruction, and many are those who enter through it. For narrow is the gate and constricted is the road that leads to life, and few are those who find it.
translation.original_text: εισελθατε δια της στενης πυλης · οτι πλατεια η πυλη και ευρυχωρος η οδος η απαγουσα εις την απωλειαν

translation.text: Therefore everyone who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house upon the rock. And the rain came down, and the floods came, and the winds blew and beat against that house, and it did not fall, for it had been founded upon the rock.
translation.original_text: πας ουν οστις ακουει μου τους λογους τουτους και ποιει αυτους · ομοιωθησεται ανδρι φρονιμω

translator_notes.note: The image of two paths—one leading to life, one to destruction—echoes Deuteronomy 30:19 and the Didache’s “Two Ways” teaching. The Greek stene (στενή) means constricted or compressed, implying transformation through pressure.
